records.notes: Shelf-forming oyster on aspen and related hardwoods.
records.culinary_uses: Cooked like other oyster mushrooms: sauté, add to soups, or use in mixed mushroom dishes. Best when young and fresh before the margins dry out.
records.general_notes: Treat as a gilled wood-rotting oyster rather than 'other'. In the U.P./Upper Great Lakes this is a Populus/aspen oyster-type entry and should be compared with pearl and summer oysters; source support allows spring-through-fall framing.

substrate: Dead or living aspen, cottonwood, and other Populus wood
cap_surface: Somewhat greasy when young and fresh
mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring, summer, and fall on dead aspen/cottonwood and related Populus wood; June through November is the app's conservative active display window.

records.field_identification: Shelf-like oyster mushroom on aspen or other Populus wood, with whitish decurrent gills and little to no central stem. The spore print is whitish, not lilac; host wood is a major separator from other oyster mushrooms.
records.mushroom_card_note: A good edible oyster on aspen/poplar wood; best young and cooked.

records.look_alike_notes: Compare with pearl and summer oysters; host wood and season help. Avoid orange jack-o-lanterns, which grow from buried wood/roots and have true orange gills.
records.edibility_detail: Edible oyster mushroom after confident identification and cooking. Use the aspen/poplar host clue, decurrent white gills, white flesh, whitish spore print, and shelf-like growth habit; avoid old, waterlogged, buggy, or dried-out specimens and do not assume every pale shelf mushroom on wood is an oyster.

lookalike_risk.summary: Oyster mushrooms are safe only after confirming wood growth, decurrent pale gills, reduced/lateral stem, no ring/volva, and expected spore print. Angel Wing is the major missing caution in conifer country.

Spore print recommended
No ring or volva
Avoid conifer-wood oyster-like mushrooms unless Angel Wing is ruled out

lookalike_traits: Dense clusters from wood, roots, or buried roots
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it has true orange gills, route away from the edible target.

records.display_name: Wood blewit group / Clitocybe nuda group
records.notes: Lilac-toned forest-litter gilled mushroom.
records.culinary_uses: Good edible when properly identified and cooked; flavor is earthy/aromatic. Avoid old soggy specimens and confirm spore print/ID against purple Cortinarius look-alikes.
records.general_notes: A practical gilled entry for the guide because the color, smell, and late-season habit are all useful identification cues.

mushroom_profile.season_note: Mostly fall after cool weather begins.
cap_shape: Convex with inrolled margin when young
Wavy or uplifted margin in age
habitat: Leaf litter and organic debris in woods

records.scientific_name: Clitocybe nuda group / wood blewit complex
records.field_identification: Young mushrooms are dull purple, lilac, or lavender on the cap, gills, and stem, then fade toward buff or brown. Gills are attached, the sweet/fragrant odor can be noticeable, there is no veil, and the spore print is pinkish rather than rusty brown.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good cooked edible, but confirm it is a blewit and not a purple Cortinarius.

records.look_alike_notes: Purple Cortinarius species can look similar but have rusty-brown spore prints and often cobwebby cortina remnants. Wood blewits have a pinkish print, no cortina, and commonly grow in leaf litter or other organic debris. Color alone is not enough.
records.edibility_detail: Edible only after confident identification and cooking. Confirm the pinkish-buff spore print and absence of a Cortinarius-style rusty brown spore print/cortina; avoid raw use, old soggy specimens, and any purple-brown mushroom with rusty spores or unpleasant earthy/chemical odor.
records.quality_notes: Good earthy/aromatic edible when fresh and properly identified; older specimens fade brown and become harder to identify.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook thoroughly. Use only fresh specimens that match blewit traits and have a pale pinkish-buff spore print.
records.risk_reason: Purple Cortinarius species can look similar but have rusty-brown spores and may include dangerous kidney-toxin species.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If confused with toxic Cortinarius: delayed kidney injury risk. General misidentification can cause serious poisoning depending on species.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Purple/lilac cap, gills, and stem fade with age; older specimens can look tan/brown. Blewits have pale pinkish-buff spores and no cortina; Cortinarius has rusty-brown spores and often cobwebby veil remnants.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat a suspected blewit unless the spore print is pale pinkish, there is no cortina or rusty spore dust, and the mushroom matches fresh blewit traits.
records.caution_reason: Cortinarius look-alike risk; spore print is required.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — dangerous Cortinarius confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Blewits are not beginner-safe because purple Cortinarius/webcaps can overlap visually and some Cortinarius can damage kidneys. Purple color alone is not enough.
required_checks: Pale pinkish-buff spore print required
No rusty-brown spore print
No cortina/cobwebby veil remnants
No rusty spore dust or rusty veil remnants
Fresh lilac/purple traits may fade with age
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Rusty-brown spores or cortina remnants mean do not eat as blewit.

lookalike_traits: Cobwebby cortina or rusty veil remnants
Rusty/brown dust on gills or stem
required_checks: Spore print required
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Rusty-brown spores or cortina remnants mean do not eat as blewit.

edible_use.notes: Cook thoroughly. Use only fresh specimens that match blewit traits and have a pale pinkish-buff spore print; reject Cortinarius-like rusty spores or cortina remnants.

records.notes: Deadly white Amanita.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat. Dig up the entire base of any white-gilled mushroom; if there is a cup, sack, bulb, volva, or uncertainty, stop.
records.edibility_detail: Deadly poisonous. Not edible under any circumstance.
records.general_notes: One of the highest-priority caution mushrooms in the guide. The combination of white gills, ring, and volva matters more than almost anything else in the gilled lane.

substrate: on ground; mycorrhizal, not growing from wood
ring: present; high skirtlike annulus, but fragile specimens can lose visible ring evidence
mushroom_profile.volva: present; white sacklike cup around base, sometimes buried
staining: usually none; may age slightly yellowish or pinkish in some specimens
smell: not distinctive when young; old specimens may smell foul/sickly sweet
habitat: On ground in oak and hardwood woods

records.scientific_name: Amanita bisporigera and closely related eastern North American white destroying angels; do not use European-only names such as Amanita virosa or Amanita verna as the main regional label.
records.field_identification: All-white mushroom with white cap, white free or nearly free gills, white stem, and white flesh. Persistent high skirtlike ring on the upper stem may be present. White sacklike volva/cup at the swollen base; the base may be underground, so the whole mushroom must be dug up and checked for identification. The image set includes a base/volva example because this is a life-or-death field mark. Cap is smooth/bald, dry to slightly sticky, oval when young and convex to flat with age. Often associated with oaks and other hardwoods; fruits in summer and fall. Old specimens may develop an unpleasant sickly-sweet or rotting odor.
records.mushroom_card_note: Do not eat any white gilled mushroom with a ring or any hint of a cup/volva at the base. If the base was not dug up and checked, treat it as unsafe.

records.poisoning_effects: Delayed vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps, dehydration, apparent recovery, liver injury, kidney failure, coma, and possible death.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Destroying Angel Group. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.look_alike_notes: Meadow Mushroom Agaricus Group: Agaricus has pink to chocolate-brown mature gills and a dark brown spore print, not white gills and a white spore print; young buttons can still fool people. Puffballs: Young Amanita buttons can resemble puffballs before the cap opens. Slice any puffball-like mushroom top-to-bottom; any outline of cap, gills, stem, ring, or volva means reject. Volvopluteus Stubble Rosegill: Volvopluteus has pink mature gills and pink spore print and lacks a ring; destroying angels keep white gills/spores and have ring plus volva.

records.toxicity_notes: Contains amatoxins with delayed GI illness, apparent recovery, then possible liver and kidney failure.
records.caution_reason: Amatoxin risk; white-gilled Amanitas can kill and the buried volva/base is often missed.
records.risk_reason: Contains amatoxins. A person may feel fine for hours, then develop severe gastrointestinal symptoms, seem to improve, and later develop liver and kidney failure.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Liver and kidneys; delayed vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal cramps, dehydration, apparent recovery, liver injury, kidney failure, coma, and possible death.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: White gills, white spore print, possible ring, and a cup-like volva at the base that may be buried in soil or leaf litter. Young button-stage Amanitas can be mistaken for puffballs or edible white mushrooms if not cut open and dug up completely.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat any white-gilled mushroom with a volva, cup, sack, bulb, or unknown buried base. Dig up the entire base; if Amanita traits are present or uncertain, do not eat.
records.danger_notes: Delayed symptoms are more dangerous than quick stomach upset. If vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain begins 6 or more hours after eating wild mushrooms, treat it as a medical emergency and contact Poison Control or emergency care.

field_measurements.stem: 5.5–14 cm long; 0.5–2 cm thick / about 2.2–5.5 in long; 0.2–0.8 in thick
field_measurements.notes: Size source: MushroomExpert for Amanita bisporigera, used as the eastern destroying-angel anchor. Check the enlarged base/volva; the base may be underground.

records.look_alike_notes: Do not use vague white lawn mushrooms. Deadly Amanitas have white gills/spores and a volva; yellow-staining Agaricus types stain bright yellow and often smell chemical/phenolic.
records.quality_notes: Store-mushroom-like food value when the species is good; older specimens get dark, buggy, or unpleasant.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook thoroughly. Do not use any white lawn mushroom unless gill color, spore print, base, ring, bruising, odor, and habitat all fit the edible Agaricus target.
records.risk_reason: Yellow-staining/phenolic Agaricus can cause GI poisoning, and deadly Amanitas can be confused with white lawn mushrooms if gills, spores, base, ring, odor, and staining are not checked.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Yellow-staining Agaricus: gastrointestinal tract with nausea, cramps, diarrhea, and vomiting. Amanita confusion: liver and kidney failure risk.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Free gills that start pink and become chocolate-brown, brown spore print, ring, no volva, staining reaction, and odor all matter. Bright yellow staining at the stem base or phenolic/ink/iodine smell is a stop sign.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat Agaricus with bright yellow staining at the stem base or a chemical/phenolic odor. Do not eat white-gilled mushrooms unless Amanita has been ruled out by digging up the entire base.
records.caution_reason: Yellow-staining/phenolic Agaricus can cause GI poisoning, and deadly Amanitas can be confused with white lawn mushrooms if gills, spores, base, ring, odor, and staining are not checked.

edible_use.notes: Cook only after careful Agaricus identification. Confirm pink-to-chocolate gills, brown spore print, no volva, no bright yellow base staining, and no phenolic/ink-like odor.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with caution for experienced foragers only. Confirm a white spore print, wood/root growth, pale attached-to-decurrent gills, and Armillaria traits. Cook thoroughly; avoid old specimens and try a small amount first.
records.general_notes: Honey mushrooms are a species complex. The combination of clustered growth from wood/roots, white spores, ring/rhizomorph clues, and lack of orange glow helps separate them from jack-o-lanterns.

edible_use.notes: Cook completely and start with a small serving. Many foragers parboil or blanch first. This is an edible-with-caution record because of toxic look-alikes and individual GI reactions, not because the food value is automatically poor.

records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style growth on wood, roots, or buried wood. Deadly galerina has rusty-brown spores and small brown caps; sulfur tuft has yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores; brick cap has gray to purple-gray gills and brick-red caps; shaggy scalycap has heavy scales and cinnamon-brown spores; jack-o-lanterns are orange, clustered on wood, and poisonous. Do not eat honey mushrooms unless a mature cap gives a white spore print; deadly galerina can grow on the same wood and has a rusty-brown spore print, while jack-o’-lanterns can share the white spore print but have bright orange flesh and true orange gills.
records.foraging_value: Fair to good edible for experienced foragers only
records.caution_reason: Look-alike risk with deadly Galerina and jack-o’-lantern, plus real GI intolerance in some people.
records.flavor_texture_notes: Best young and firm; older caps and stems can be tough, buggy, or trigger individual reactions.
records.quality_notes: Rich, slightly sweet, sometimes mucilaginous; young tight caps are best.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook completely; many foragers parboil or blanch first. Start with a small serving.
records.risk_reason: Honey mushrooms overlap in habitat and season with deadly Galerina and poisonous jack-o’-lanterns. Correctly identified honey mushrooms also bother some stomachs.
records.symptoms_body_systems: If honey mushroom intolerance: gastrointestinal upset. If confused with Galerina: delayed amatoxin poisoning affecting liver and kidneys. If confused with jack-o’-lantern: cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Wood-associated growth, tan/yellow-brown caps, clustered fruiting, variable ring presence, and novice confusion with other wood-growing mushrooms. White spore print supports honey mushroom; rusty-brown spore print points away from honey and toward Galerina danger.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat honey mushrooms without a white spore print and positive Armillaria/Desarmillaria identification. Any rusty-brown spore print, sharp orange gills, or uncertainty means do not eat.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — deadly wood-mushroom confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Honey mushrooms can be good food for experienced foragers, but Deadly Galerina and other clustered wood mushrooms are similar enough that spore print and specimen-by-specimen checks are mandatory.

Confirm Armillaria/Desarmillaria traits
Check every specimen in mixed wood clusters
Reject rusty-brown, cinnamon, purple-brown, greenish, or dark spores
Reject bright pumpkin-orange flesh or sharp true gills
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Treat every honey mushroom collection specimen-by-specimen. One wrong wood mushroom ruins the basket.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Deadly — amatoxin poisoning can damage liver and kidneys after delayed symptoms.
dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Deadly Galerina can grow on wood near honey mushrooms; spore print color is a stop-sign check.

Each specimen in a cluster matches the honey-mushroom group being used

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Any rusty-brown spore print means do not eat.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Jack-o’-lanterns can overlap with clustered wood-growing mushrooms and are poisonous.

lookalike_traits: Dense clusters from wood, stumps, or buried roots
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not eat orange clustered wood mushrooms as honey mushrooms.

records.scientific_name: Omphalotus illudens, the eastern North American jack-o’-lantern mushroom.
records.poisoning_effects: Painful stomach cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_identification: Bright orange to pumpkin-orange mushroom, usually in clusters. Grows from hardwood stumps, buried roots, or dead/dying hardwood wood; can appear to grow from the ground when attached to buried wood. True gills are sharp-edged, close/crowded, orange, and run down the stem. Flesh is orange or orangish when sliced. Spore print is white to creamy or pale yellow. Fresh gills can glow faintly green in the dark, but glow is not reliable enough to use as the main ID trait.
records.overview: Searchable danger entry for Jack-o’-Lantern. Included because it can be confused with edible mushrooms in Upper Michigan / northern Great Lakes foraging contexts.
records.look_alike_notes: Chanterelles: Chanterelles grow singly or scattered from the forest floor and have blunt false gills/ridges, not sharp true gills. Jack-o’-lanterns usually cluster on wood or buried roots. False Chanterelle: False chanterelle is also orange with true gill-like structures but is generally smaller/flimsier and not the same clustered wood-rotting jack-o’-lantern hazard.

records.mushroom_card_note: Do not eat orange clustered mushrooms from wood or buried roots. True gills plus orange clusters equals no, even if it looks chanterelle-ish.
records.toxicity_notes: Poisonous gastrointestinal irritant. It commonly causes painful cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea after chanterelle misidentification; symptoms usually resolve without lasting injury, but medical care may be needed.
records.risk_reason: Commonly mistaken for chanterelles because of the orange/yellow-orange color and decurrent gill-like underside.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract; painful stomach cramps, vomiting, and diarrhea.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Grows in clusters from rotting wood, stumps, or buried roots and has true, sharp-edged gills. Chanterelles grow from soil and have blunt forked ridges/false gills.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat orange “chanterelles” growing in clusters from wood or with sharp true gills.
records.caution_reason: Poisonous chanterelle look-alike; wood growth and true gills are stop signs.

records.culinary_uses: Cook young, clean specimens only after confident ID. Discard anything with chemical/phenolic smell, strong yellow staining, or uncertain base.
records.edibility_detail: Edible only after careful Agaricus identification. Beginners should avoid any white mushroom until they can rule out Amanita: check gill color, spore print, stem base, ring, bruising, and odor.
records.general_notes: Use this as a meadow/field Agaricus complex rather than claiming European Agaricus campestris as a confirmed Upper Michigan species. Similar eastern North American collections often require microscopy or DNA for species-level identification. Keep the deadly Amanita contrast visible because white lawn mushrooms are a common mistake zone.

records.caution_reason: Yellow-staining/phenolic Agaricus can cause GI poisoning, and deadly Amanitas can be confused with white lawn mushrooms if gills, spores, base, ring, odor, and staining are not checked.

records.overview: Bulbous honey mushrooms in the Great Lakes region include the Armillaria gallica–A. calvescens complex. They usually fruit from hardwood roots, buried wood, or near tree bases, often in loose groups, with a swollen stem base and a cobwebby or short-lived ring zone. The two species are not reliably separated in the field.
records.notes: Species-level split for the honey mushroom complex so the app does not treat all honeys as one vague bucket.
records.general_notes: Species-level split for the honey mushroom complex so the app does not treat all honeys as one vague bucket.
records.field_identification: Look for tan to pinkish-brown caps with small yellowish-brown scales, whitish gills running slightly down the stem, white spore print, swollen stem base, a yellowish cobwebby or flimsy ring zone, and attachment to buried roots or wood. In Upper Michigan, do not claim A. gallica versus A. calvescens from field traits alone.
records.foraging_value: Fair to good edible for experienced foragers only

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Armillaria gallica
use_links.notes: Bulbous base, loose growth, late season, white spores, ring zone, and hardwood/root association.

records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style growth on wood, roots, or buried wood. Deadly galerina has rusty-brown spores and small brown caps; sulfur tuft has yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores; brick cap has gray to purple-gray gills and brick-red caps; shaggy scalycap has heavy scales and cinnamon-brown spores; jack-o-lanterns are orange, clustered on wood, and poisonous. Do not eat honey mushrooms unless a mature cap gives a white spore print; deadly galerina can grow on the same wood and has a rusty-brown spore print, while jack-o’-lanterns can share the white spore print but have bright orange flesh and true orange gills.
records.caution_reason: Look-alike risk and GI intolerance risk; not a beginner shortcut mushroom.
records.flavor_texture_notes: Quality is best in young firm caps; stems can be fibrous.

records.edibility_detail: Edible with caution when young and fresh. Cook thoroughly and try only a small first portion because undercooked ringless honeys can cause serious stomach upset and individual reactions occur.
records.culinary_uses: Use only young, fresh, confidently identified specimens. Cook thoroughly; many foragers discard the tough stems and use the caps. Avoid mushrooms from chemically treated lawns.
records.overview: Ringless honey mushrooms are densely clustered, white-spored wood/root mushrooms without a true stem ring. The current eastern North American name used by the Missouri Department of Conservation is Desarmillaria caespitosa; older sources often use Armillaria tabescens.
records.notes: Ringless honey group added to separate no-ring honey mushrooms from classic ringed Armillaria and to improve gilled mushroom coverage.
records.general_notes: Ringless honey group added to separate no-ring honey mushrooms from classic ringed Armillaria and to improve gilled mushroom coverage.
records.field_identification: Confirm dense clusters from hardwood stumps, tree bases, roots, or buried wood; tan to honey-brown caps with small scales when young; pale gills that attach to or run slightly down the stem; fused stem bases; no true ring; and a white spore print. The missing ring does not make a clustered mushroom automatically safe.
records.commonness: Needs regional review; more southern/warm-region tendency
records.foraging_value: Fair edible for experienced foragers only

records.look_alike_notes: Honey mushrooms have white spores and Armillaria-style growth on wood, roots, or buried wood. Deadly galerina has rusty-brown spores and small brown caps; sulfur tuft has yellow-green gills and purple-brown spores; brick cap has gray to purple-gray gills and brick-red caps; shaggy scalycap has heavy scales and cinnamon-brown spores; jack-o-lanterns are orange, clustered on wood, and poisonous. Do not eat honey mushrooms unless a mature cap gives a white spore print; deadly galerina can grow on the same wood and has a rusty-brown spore print, while jack-o’-lanterns can share the white spore print but have bright orange flesh and true orange gills.
records.caution_reason: Look-alike risk and GI intolerance risk; not a beginner shortcut mushroom.

records.field_identification: Look for tight clusters on decaying hardwood logs and stumps; brick-red cap center with paler pinkish-buff margin; attached gray to purple-gray gills; yellowish-white stem that may bruise or stain yellow; purple-brown spore print.
records.culinary_uses: Recognition and comparison only. Do not consume from this entry.
records.edibility_detail: Recognition-only HOLD. Source treatment is inconsistent, and clustered wood look-alikes include poisonous sulfur tuft and deadly Galerina. Do not consume from this entry.

records.overview: Brown-capped, pink-spored Pluteus mushrooms on dead wood. In the Upper Michigan transition to boreal forest, deer-mushroom collections may belong to Pluteus rangifer rather than true P. cervinus, and field traits do not always separate the cryptic species.
records.notes: Brown-capped wood mushroom with free gills that start pale and become pink as spores mature. Included as a common gilled wood mushroom and pink-spored teaching entry.
records.general_notes: Source/status cleanup v4.3.170: MushroomExpert supports ID traits, but edible-use clearance still needs stronger non-Wikipedia support. Keep visible, food-valid with expert-ID caution, and review-needed.
records.field_identification: Confirm growth from dead wood or buried wood, gills completely free from the stem, gills changing from white to pink, a brownish-pink spore print, no ring or volva, and a brown often radially streaked cap. Treat the exact species name as provisional without microscopy or sequencing.
records.culinary_uses: Recognition and comparison only. Do not consume from this entry.
records.edibility_detail: Recognition-only HOLD. Exact northern species identity and safety clearance remain unresolved.
records.foraging_value: Low food value; expert-ID teaching mushroom; review needed
records.look_alike_notes: Deer mushrooms have free gills that turn pink and a brownish-pink spore print. Entoloma species can also become pink-spored, but their gills attach to the stem rather than staying free. Destroying angels and death caps have white spores plus Amanita base/ring features, and they grow from the ground rather than from dead wood. Dig up any pale-gilled mushroom base before ruling out Amanita.

records.symptoms_body_systems: Risk depends on misidentification; confusion with unrelated gilled mushrooms can cause serious poisoning.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Brown/tan/gray cap on dead or buried wood, free gills turning pink, salmon-pink spore print, no ring, and no volva. Similar pink-spored Entoloma species have attached gills; Amanitas have white spores plus base/ring features.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat unless confidently keyed as Pluteus, including free gills, pink spore print, wood substrate, no ring, and no volva.
source_review_notes: MushroomExpert supports brownish-pink print, free gills, deadwood growth, and a P. cervinus cryptic-species complex.
MushroomExpert notes P. rangifer may replace P. cervinus in boreal and transitional forests, making the exact Upper Michigan name uncertain.
Food routing is held until strong edible-use support is tied to the correctly framed northern species group.

records.overview: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.notes: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.general_notes: Small tan grassland mushroom famous for arcs or rings, but many unrelated mushrooms also form fairy rings. Included as an advanced edible-with-caution and cautionary lawn-mushroom entry.
records.field_identification: Look for small pale tan to buff caps in grass, well-spaced white to buff gills that attach to the stem but do not run down it, a white spore print, and a tough pliant stem that bends rather than snapping easily. Fairy-ring growth alone is not enough for identification.
records.culinary_uses: Use only expertly confirmed caps from untreated grass. The tough stems are commonly discarded. Cook before eating and try a small first portion.
records.edibility_detail: Edible, but advanced because many unrelated and poisonous lawn mushrooms also form rings. Confirm the full trait set rather than the ring pattern: grass habitat, pale tan cap, widely spaced pale gills, tough pliant stem, and white spore print.

edible_use.notes: Advanced edible-with-caution. The common name causes confusion because many mushrooms grow in rings.

records.foraging_value: Good edible for experienced identifiers; not a beginner lawn mushroom
records.look_alike_notes: Do not use the ring pattern alone: green-spored parasol, meadow mushrooms, and poisonous pale funnels can also appear in grass or rings. Fairy ring mushroom is smaller, tan, white-spored, has well-spaced gills, and a tough pliant stem; fool’s funnel has more crowded decurrent gills and muscarine risk.
